title: ζάω
content:
ζάω

ζάω (G2198)

impf. ἔζην | ἀναζάω · ἀναζωπυρέω · ζωγρέω · ζωή · ζῳογονέω · ζῷον · συζάω · συζωοποιέω

23.88 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to be alive, to live to be in existence, having life, not dead In some figurative expressions ζάω[a] and ζωή may involve serious complications in reference. For example, in JHN 6:51 the expression ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ζῶν ‘I am the living bread’ may be understood in some languages as bread which has some living objects in it, namely, bread which is being eaten by worms or weevils. It may therefore be necessary to say ‘I am that bread which gives life.’1
In a number of languages there is no noun-like word for ‘life.’ It may therefore be necessary to change all passages which speak of ‘life’ into a corresponding expression using a verb-like form meaning ‘to live.’

MAT 4:4 · MAT 16:16 · MAT 22:32 · MAT 26:63 · MAT 27:63 · MRK 5:23 · MRK 12:27 · LUK 2:36 · LUK 4:4 · LUK 10:28 · LUK 20:38 · LUK 20:38 · LUK 24:5 · JHN 4:10 · JHN 4:11 · JHN 4:50 · JHN 4:51 · JHN 4:53 · JHN 6:51 · JHN 6:51 · JHN 6:57 · JHN 6:57 · JHN 6:57 · JHN 6:58 · JHN 7:38 · JHN 11:26 · JHN 14:19 · JHN 14:19 · ACT 7:38 · ACT 10:42 · ACT 14:15 · ACT 17:28 · ACT 22:22 · ACT 25:24 · ACT 28:4 · ROM 1:17 · ROM 6:13 · ROM 7:1 · ROM 7:2 · ROM 7:3 · ROM 7:9 · ROM 8:13 · ROM 9:26 · ROM 10:5 · ROM 12:1 · ROM 14:7 · ROM 14:8 · ROM 14:8 · ROM 14:8 · ROM 14:9 · ROM 14:11 · 1CO 7:39 · 1CO 9:14 · 1CO 15:45 · 2CO 1:8 · 2CO 3:3 · 2CO 4:11 · 2CO 5:15 · 2CO 5:15 · 2CO 6:9 · 2CO 6:16 · 2CO 13:4 · GAL 3:11 · GAL 3:12 · GAL 5:25 · PHP 1:21 · PHP 1:22 · 1TH 1:9 · 1TH 3:8 · 1TH 4:15 · 1TH 4:17 · 1TH 5:10 · 1TI 3:15 · 1TI 4:10 · 1TI 5:6 · 2TI 4:1 · HEB 2:15 · HEB 3:12 · HEB 4:12 · HEB 7:8 · HEB 7:25 · HEB 9:14 · HEB 9:17 · HEB 10:20 · HEB 10:31 · HEB 10:38 · HEB 12:9 · HEB 12:22 · JAS 4:15 · 1PE 1:3 · 1PE 1:23 · 1PE 2:4 · 1PE 2:5 · 1PE 4:5 · 1PE 4:6 · 1JN 4:9 · REV 1:18 · REV 1:18 · REV 3:1 · REV 4:9 · REV 4:10 · REV 7:2 · REV 10:6 · REV 13:14 · REV 15:7 · REV 19:20

23.93 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to come back to life, to live again, to be resurrected, resurrection to come back to life after having once died In a number of languages there is a difficulty involved in formulating some expression for ‘resurrection’ or ‘living again,’ since such a phrase may refer to what is technically known as metempsychosis, that is to say, the rebirth of the soul in another existence, a belief which is widely held in a number of areas of south Asia. This problem may be avoided in some languages by speaking of ‘his body will live again’ or ‘his body will come back to life’ or ‘he will be the same person when he lives again.’

MAT 9:18 · MRK 16:11 · LUK 15:32 · LUK 24:23 · JHN 5:25 · JHN 11:25 · ACT 1:3 · ACT 9:41 · ACT 20:12 · ACT 25:19 · ROM 14:9 · 2CO 13:4 · REV 2:8 · REV 20:4 · REV 20:5

41.2 · Behavior and Related States · Behavior, Conduct

to live, to conduct oneself, to behave to conduct oneself, with the particular manner specified by the context In 2TI 3:12 ζάω does not refer to physiological existence, but rather to the manner in which a person behaves or conducts himself in relationship to other persons and to God.

LUK 15:13 · ACT 26:5 · ROM 6:2 · ROM 6:10 · ROM 6:10 · ROM 6:11 · ROM 8:12 · ROM 8:13 · GAL 2:14 · GAL 2:19 · GAL 2:20 · GAL 2:20 · GAL 2:20 · GAL 2:20 · COL 2:20 · COL 3:7 · 2TI 3:12 · TIT 2:12 · 1PE 2:24

1 The phrase ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ζῶν is probably best interpreted as a type of transform of the phrase ὁ ἄρτος τῆς ζωῆς in JHN 6:48 and accordingly may be best translated as ‘bread that gives life’ or ‘bread that causes life.’

title: ζεστός
content:
ζεστός

ζεστός (G2200)

adj | -ή, -όν | ζέω

79.71 · Features of Objects · Hot, Lukewarm, Cold

hot pertaining to being hot, often associated with boiling In REV 3:15 the usage is figurative; ζεστός in the sense of a favorable attitude towards something seems not to have been a conventionalized meaning.
In a number of languages the contrast between ‘hot’ and ‘cold’ may refer primarily to sex or anger, in which case the contrast is certainly not appropriate for REV 3:15. It may therefore be necessary in many instances to use an equivalent such as ‘for or against’ or ‘friendly to or hostile toward.’

to be earnest, to set one’s heart on, to be completely intent upon to be deeply committed to something, with the implication of accompanying desire It is also possible to understand ζηλοῦτε in 1CO 12:31 as being indicative and therefore translated as ‘you set your hearts on the more important gifts.’

title: ζηλωτής
content:
ζηλωτής

ζηλωτής (G2208)

noun | -οῦ | ζηλόω

25.77 · Attitudes and Emotions · Be Eager, Be Earnest, In a Devoted Manner

enthusiast, zealous person one who is deeply committed to something and therefore zealous

ACT 21:20 · ACT 22:3 · 1CO 14:12 · GAL 1:14 · TIT 2:14 · 1PE 3:13

11.88 · Groups and Classes of Persons and Members of Such Groups and Classes · Socio-Political

zealot, nationalist a member of a Jewish nationalistic group seeking independence from Rome It is possible that ζηλωτής in LUK 6:15 and Καναναῖος in MAT 10:4 function not so much as a designation of an individual belonging to the particular Jewish nationalistic party (according to some scholars such a party may not have been organized at that time), but of one whjo is simply zealous for national independence (see ζηλωτής[a] ‘enthusiast, zealous person,’ 25.77). Therefore, one might speak of Simon as ‘Simon the patriot’ (the same applies to ACT 1:13, the only other occurrence of ζηλωτής[b] in the NT). To render ζηλωτής[b] as ‘armed insurrectionist’ or ‘guerilla fighter’ (as some translators have suggested) would be reading into the text more than is specifically warranted.

LUK 6:15 · ACT 1:13

title: ζυγός
content:
ζυγός

ζυγός (G2218)

noun | -οῦ

6.8 · Artifacts · Instruments Used in Agriculture and Husbandry

yoke a bar or frame of wood by which two draft animals are joined at the head or neck in order to work together effectively in pulling a plow, harrow, or wagon ζυγός[a] occurs in the NT only in figurative contexts. In a sense, this means that ζυγός is itself figurative in meaning, but the figure of speech is an extended figure in which case individual components are normally regarded as having a literal meaning.

MAT 11:29 · MAT 11:30 · ACT 15:10 · GAL 5:1 · 1TI 6:1

6.214 · Artifacts · Instruments for Measuring

balance scale an instrument for weighing objects A balance scale may be described in some languages as ‘a tool for weighing’ or ‘an instrument for knowing how much something weighs.’ Ancient balance scales often consisted of a rod held by a cord in the middle and with pans attached to both ends. Weights could be placed in one pan, while the item to be weighed would be placed in the other.

REV 6:5

title: ζύμη
content:
ζύμη

ζύμη (G2219)

noun | -ης

5.11 · Foods and Condiments · Food

yeast leaven employed in making bread rise In some languages yeast is described simply as ‘that which makes bread rise,’ but frequently yeast is described in terms of some characteristic or on the basis of its source, for example, ‘sour froth’ or ‘beer foam.’

MAT 13:33 · MAT 16:12 · LUK 13:21 · 1CO 5:6 · GAL 5:9

88.237 · Moral and Ethical Qualities and Related Behavior · Hypocrisy, Pretense

hypocrisy, pretense (a figurative extension of meaning of ζύμη[a] ‘yeast,’ 5.11) hypocritical behavior, probably implying hidden attitudes and motivations In MAT 16:6 the ζύμη of the Pharisees and the Sadducees is equated with their teaching, but the context would indicate clearly that this was a type of hypocritical teaching, since the religious leaders said one thing but did something quite different.
In 1CO 5:6 1CO 5:7 there is an extended figurative reference to ζύμη. At first, ζύμη must be understood in the literal sense (as in Domain 5), yet ζύμη in verse 7 refers to wrong behavior. It is important, however, to try to preserve the continuity of the figurative reference, and for that reason the phrase παλαιὰ ζύμη is sometimes translated as ‘the old yeast of sin.’ It is difficult to know to what extent there may be a reference at this point to the hypocrisy which accompanies legalistic behavior.

MAT 16:6 · MAT 16:11 · MRK 8:15 · MRK 8:15 · LUK 12:1 · 1CO 5:7 · 1CO 5:8 · 1CO 5:8

ζυμόω

ἄζυμος · ζύμη

to use yeast (derivative of ζύμη ‘yeast,’ 5.11) to employ yeast in the process of making bread rise

MAT 13:33 · LUK 13:21 · 1CO 5:6 · GAL 5:9

title: ζωγρέω
content:
ζωγρέω

ζωγρέω (G2221)

ζάω + ἄγρα

37.1 · Control, Rule · Control, Restrain

to control, to bring under control, to hold in check to bring under control and to continue to restrain In many languages it may be difficult to speak of ‘bringing a person under control.’ It may therefore be more appropriate to use some such phrase as ‘not allow a person to do just what he wants’ or ‘make a person behave’ or ‘make a person obey.’ In speaking of animals, one may render JAS 3:7 as ‘all kinds of wild animals ... can be made to obey a person.’

LUK 5:10 · 2TI 2:26

title: ζωή
content:
ζωή

ζωή (G2222)

noun | -ῆς | ζάω

23.88 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

life the condition of being alive and well because of the presence of air to breathe and food to eat; in many cases its usage implies health, strength, and some degree of prosperity In some figurative expressions ζάω[a] and ζωή may involve serious complications in reference. For example, in JHN 6:51 the expression ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ζῶν ‘I am the living bread’ may be understood in some languages as bread which has some living objects in it, namely, bread which is being eaten by worms or weevils. It may therefore be necessary to say ‘I am that bread which gives life.’1
In MRK 10:30, καὶ ἐν τῷ αἰῶνι τῷ ἐρχομένῳ ζωὴν αἰώνιον ‘and in the age to come (he will receive) eternal life,’ there are significant differences of opinion as to whether the emphasis is primarily upon duration or upon the quality of life which is to be characteristic of the coming age. Since in some languages life can only be spoken of as a verb meaning literally ‘to live,’ then ‘eternal life’ might be expressed merely as ‘living without dying,’ but this can be misunderstood to mean that Christians will never die physically. Some translators have thus spoken of ‘eternal life’ as being ‘real life that never ends.’ In this way a qualitative distinction is introduced so that readers will not think merely in terms of the prolongation of earthly life, something which in some parts of the world would be regarded as being a terrible punishment rather than a blessing.
In a number of languages there is no noun-like word for ‘life.’ It may therefore be necessary to change all passages which speak of ‘life’ into a corresponding expression using a verb-like form meaning ‘to live.’

1 The phrase ὁ ἄρτος ὁ ζῶν is probably best interpreted as a type of transform of the phrase ὁ ἄρτος τῆς ζωῆς in JHN 6:48 and accordingly may be best translated as ‘bread that gives life’ or ‘bread that causes life.’

title: ζώνη
content:
ζώνη

ζώνη (G2223)

noun | -ης | ζώννυμι

6.178 · Artifacts · Cloth, Leather, and Objects Made of Such Materials

belt, girdle a band of leather or cloth worn around the waist outside of one’s clothing A ζώνη was normally quite wide and could be readily folded. As such, it was often used to carry money (see MAT 10:9).

MAT 3:4 · MAT 10:9 · MRK 1:6 · MRK 6:8 · ACT 21:11 · ACT 21:11 · REV 1:13 · REV 15:6

title: ζωννύω
content:
ζωννύω

ζωννύω (G2224) — ζώννυμι

διαζώννυμι · ζώνη · περιζώννυμι · περιζωννύω · ὑποζώννυμι

49.14 · (ζώννυμι[a]) · Activities Involving Clothing and Adorning

to gird, to fasten one’s belt, to wear a narrow band of cloth around the waist, to tie around the waist to tuck up or hold a garment firmly in place by wrapping a belt, girdle, or piece of cloth around it1

ACT 12:8

49.8 · (ζώννυμι[b]) · Activities Involving Clothing and Adorning

to dress to dress oneself, including the fastening of one’s belt as the final act in dressing

JHN 21:182 · JHN 21:182 · ACT 12:8

1 It is possible that διαζώννυμι is somewhat more forceful in meaning than ζώννυμι[a], but this cannot be determined from existing contexts. 2 JHN 21:18 · JHN 21:18 In JHN 21:18 ζώννυμι involves more than the fastening of one’s belt or putting a sash around the waist. Accordingly, it differs in meaning from ζώννυμι[a] (49.14).

title: ζῳογονέω
content:
ζῳογονέω

ζῳογονέω (G2225)

ζάω + γίνομαι

23.89 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to keep alive, to preserve alive to cause to continue to live ‘So that they could not be kept alive’ (ACT 7:19) may be rendered as ‘so that they would not continue to live’ or ‘so that they would die.’

LUK 17:33 · ACT 7:19

23.92 · Physiological Processes and States · Live, Die

to give life to, to make live to cause to live

1TI 6:13

title: ζῷον
content:
ζῷον

ζῷον (G2226)

noun | -ου | ζάω

4.2 · Animals · Animals

living creature, animal any living creature, whether wild or domesticated (in contrast with plants) In a number of languages the equivalent of ‘living things’ or ‘living creatures’ is simply ‘those which are alive’ or ‘those that move about.’ ζῷον[a], therefore, includes essentially quadrupeds, fish, birds, and insects, and is not used in the NT to designate human beings. In several passages in the book of Revelation, ζῷον[b] ‘a living being’ (12.32) is employed to refer to supernatural beings at God’s throne (note REV 4:6-REV 4:9 which in many ways is similar to the description of ‘living creatures’ in EZK 1:5f).

HEB 13:11 · 2PE 2:12 · JUD 1:10

12.32 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

living being, living creature a supernatural being surrounding the throne of God in visions of the book of Revelation The OT parallel for the ζῷον in Revelation is to be found in EZK 1:5-EZK 1:14.

REV 4:6 · REV 4:7 · REV 4:7 · REV 4:7 · REV 4:7 · REV 4:8 · REV 4:9 · REV 5:6 · REV 5:8 · REV 5:11 · REV 5:14 · REV 6:1 · REV 6:3 · REV 6:5 · REV 6:6 · REV 6:7 · REV 7:11 · REV 14:3 · REV 15:7 · REV 19:4
